items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.
It's stays constant. “rises steadily” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Pressure of a gas increases when:
items.why: Correct answer: temperature rises. “it cools” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Water boils at what temperature in degrees C at standard pressure?
items.why: The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
